root.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Common Intake Guide
root.summary: Front matter and full contents for the Cúram 8.1.2 Common Intake Guide. It introduces Common Intake (CCI) as a configurable “no wrong door” intake framework for matching client needs to programs and community services, aimed at business analysts in social enterprise organizations. The guide covers the end-to-end intake flow: client search and registration, triage, screening for potential eligibility, application creation and management, life events, referrals, and case worker tools such as advisor, notes, PCR, and alerts. It also explains how agencies configure intake processes, including triage, screening, applications, life events, PCR rules/ranges, and referrals. The section includes the table of contents plus legal notices, privacy policy, and trademark information, with copyright and usage terms from Merative US L.P. (2012, 2024).

children.title: Note
children.summary: This note instructs readers to review the “Notices” section on page 59 before using the document or the product it supports. It also shows the copyright notice for Merative US L.P. covering 2012 and 2024.

children.title: Edition
Edition — pages 5-14
children.summary: Front matter for the Cúram 8.1.2 Common Intake Guide edition page range. States the edition applies to Cúram 8.1, 8.1.1, and 8.1.2, with Merative copyright/trademark notices. The section also begins the table of contents and the “Common Intake Guide” chapter, outlining the guide’s purpose, audience, prerequisites, and major topics: intake overview, client identification, triage, screening, applications, life events, referrals, case worker tools, and intake process configuration.

children.title: Edition — pages 15-24
children.summary: Covers Common Intake participant handling and intake flow: registering anonymous, prospect, and person records; merging duplicates; maintaining person/prospect data; and creating/linking Citizen Accounts. Introduces triage as a quick needs assessment for anonymous/prospect/registered clients, with results, alerts, provider mapping, referrals, and program recommendations. Also explains screening outcomes and notes, then begins the application lifecycle: starting applications from triage/screening, capturing evidence, saving/resuming, submitting via multiple channels, routing, and recording application details and client information.

children.title: Edition — pages 25-34
children.summary: Covers the full application lifecycle in Common Intake: searching/matching/registering clients, adding/removing clients, transferring or adding applications to existing cases, scheduling interviews, managing evidence and verifications, running eligibility checks, PCR assessment triggers, notes/attachments/communications, processing timers, authorizing/declining benefits, handling appeals, establishing cases, and application/program status transitions through disposed. Useful for understanding how submitted applications are processed and how client, evidence, eligibility, and case management work together.

children.title: Edition — pages 35-44
children.summary: Covers CCI intake configuration and workflows for life events, referrals, case worker tools, and triage/map setup. Explains how life events are captured for clients with or without active cases, transmitted via evidence broker, and used to recommend programs/services, alerts, notes, and referrals. Describes referral creation, provider/agency selection, and open referral management. Details advisor, notes, PCR, alerts, and home page queues. Also explains configuring intake processes and Google Maps/geocoding properties, API key setup, default map center, and service area zip codes.

children.title: Edition — pages 45-54
children.summary: This section explains Cúram setup and workflow for Universal Access and provider/service administration, including adding zip codes to service areas, defining geocoding bias areas, assigning services and service groups, enrolling providers and provider groups, approving provider services, and linking providers to groups. It also shows how Google Maps appears in provider and triage screens. The latter pages introduce CCI screening and application configuration concepts: single vs. multiple screenings, application routing, case/evidence mapping, program recommendations, status handling, reopen rules, timers, and application-to-case creation scenarios.

children.title: Edition — pages 55-58
children.summary: This section covers CCI intake configuration topics: notifying clients of eligibility determinations, defining life events (catalog, categories, IEG scripts, CER rules, evidence broker updates), PCR setup (rules, events/manual triggers, classifications, ranges, responses), and referrals to community/external agencies. It concludes with a summary of CCI’s intake capabilities and a list of related guides for broader context.

Front matter and full contents for the Cúram 8.1.2 Common Intake Guide, plus the guide’s purpose, audience, and related references. The section introduces Common Intake (CCI) as a configurable “no wrong door” intake process for matching client needs to programs and services, then covers the end-to-end flow: client identification, triage, screening, applications, life events, referrals, case worker tools, and intake process configuration. It also outlines configuration topics for maps, provider/service setup, screening/application routing, PCR, life events, referrals, and eligibility notifications.

children.title: Notices
Privacy policy
children.summary: Contains legal and licensing notices: fictitious example names disclaimer, sample program copyright license and “AS IS” warranty/liability limits, the Merative privacy policy URL, and a trademarks/copyright list for Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle, Linux, Microsoft, and UNIX.

children.title: Trademarks
children.summary: This section lists trademark and copyright notices for Merative and third-party brands. It states that Merative and its logo are trademarks, and identifies trademarks of IBM, Adobe, Oracle/Java, Linux, Microsoft, and UNIX, plus a note that other names may be trademarks of others. It also includes a privacy policy link and a copyright notice for Merative US L.P. (2012, 2024).

Legal notices for use of the publication: personal and enterprise reproduction is allowed only if proprietary notices are preserved, with no distribution or derivatives outside the stated terms without Merative consent. It disclaims warranties (“AS IS”), limits liability, notes patent rights, export-law compliance, and states third-party product info/websites are not endorsed or guaranteed. Includes © Merative US L.P. 2012, 2024.

children.title: Contents
children.summary: Table of contents for the Cúram Common Intake Guide. Lists Chapter 1 topics and page ranges, including common intake overview, identifying clients, triage, screening eligibility, application lifecycle, life events, referrals, case worker tools, and defining intake processes. Also includes front matter entries (Note, Edition) and notices/privacy/trademarks. Useful as a navigation node to locate specific intake-related procedures and reference sections.

children.title: 1 Common Intake Guide
1.1 Introduction
Purpose
children.summary: Purpose of the Common Intake Guide: it introduces Common Intake (CCI) and explains how it matches client needs to programs and community services through a configurable intake process. The guide is for business analysts in social enterprise organizations, assumes familiarity with SEM and business requirements, and is meant to be read in full rather than used as a training/user guide. It also notes prerequisite knowledge of benefit/service delivery and related guides, and previews chapters on CCI overview and identifying clients.

children.title: Audience
children.summary: Intended for business analysts in a social enterprise organization, this section explains the purpose and scope of the Common Intake Guide (CCI). It assumes familiarity with Social Enterprise Management concepts and the organization’s business requirements, and recommends prior reading of the Integrated Case Management Guide and Cúram Participant Guide. It also outlines the guide’s chapters, including Common Intake Overview and Identifying the Client, covering intake workflow, “No Wrong Door” service access, client search/registration, anonymous and prospect persons, and citizen accounts.

children.title: Prerequisites
children.summary: Prerequisites for the Common Intake Guide: readers should know how the organization delivers benefits and services to clients, and ideally have already read the Integrated Case Management Guide and the Cúram Participant Guide. This section also frames the guide’s audience as business analysts in a social enterprise organization with strong SEM and business-requirements knowledge.

children.title: Chapters in this Guide
children.summary: This section introduces Common Intake (CCI), a configurable intake process that matches client needs to programs and community services for needs-based and protection-based programs. It states the guide’s purpose, intended audience (business analysts in social enterprise organizations), and prerequisites. It also outlines the guide’s chapters: Common Intake Overview, covering CCI’s “No Wrong Door” intake approach and main components, and Identifying the Client, covering client search/registration, anonymous persons, person records, and citizen accounts.

Introduces Common Intake (CCI), a configurable intake process that matches client needs to programs and community services for needs-based and protection-based programs. It states the guide is for business analysts in social enterprise organizations, assumes familiarity with SEM and organizational business requirements, and is not a training/user guide. Readers should know how benefits and services are delivered and preferably have read the Integrated Case Management Guide and Cúram Participant Guide. It previews chapters on Common Intake Overview and Identifying the Client.

children.title: 1.2 Common Intake Overview
What is Common Intake?
Intake Process for Needs-Based Programs and Protection-Based Programs
children.summary: Explains how intake differs for needs-based vs protection-based programs. Needs-based intake typically uses triage, screening, application, and benefit authorization, while protection-based intake begins with a report of abuse/neglect and may involve allegation management, safety/risk planning, legal proceedings, and investigation. Focuses on CCI’s needs-based intake capabilities (triage, life events, screening, application, referrals), configurable intake flows, and the No Wrong Door approach for identifying client needs, referrals, eligibility screening, and priority/complexity/risk response.

children.title: Configurable Intake Process
children.summary: This section explains that CCI supports configurable intake for needs-based programs, unlike protection-based intake. Agencies can choose which intake steps to include, such as triage, life events, screening, application, and referrals, while protection-based workflows involve investigation and safety/legal actions. It also describes “No Wrong Door,” where clients enter through a universal gateway, workers identify needs upfront, and the agency may refer, screen, or take an application. PCR (Priority, Complexity, Risk) supports differential responses.

children.title: No Wrong Door
children.summary: Explains the “No Wrong Door” model in Common Intake Guide. It presents a universal gateway to community services and government programs, letting clients describe needs rather than select programs. Workers can then identify needs up front and choose the next step: referral to external agencies or community services, eligibility screening, or benefits application. It also introduces Priority, Complexity, Risk (PCR), allowing agencies to tailor responses based on urgency and client situation.

children.title: User Roles
children.summary: Describes CCI intake user roles and their responsibilities. Intake Workers are first contact and can perform triage, screening, life events, referrals, and application processing. Eligibility Workers make final benefit determinations and may manage ongoing eligibility and outcomes. Intake Worker Supervisors manage intake worker workload. Also explains that intake is flexible and optional steps are chosen based on client needs, which may include food, shelter, safety, health, finance, and education.

Common Intake (CCI) explains a configurable, agency-wide intake approach for social service programs. It contrasts needs-based intake (triage, screening, application, benefit authorization, referrals, life events) with protection-based intake (abuse/neglect reports, investigation, safety/risk planning, legal actions). CCI is mainly focused on needs-based workflows and supports collaboration through external referrals. It promotes a “No Wrong Door” model, where workers identify needs upfront and route clients by referral, screening, or application, with Priority, Complexity, Risk (PCR) guiding response.

children.title: Addressing the Client's Needs
Identifying the Client
children.summary: Explains how a worker identifies whether an approaching person is an existing client, registers new clients, or continues with anonymous intake. Covers related intake paths: triage to quickly assess immediate needs, life events to capture significant changes and update active cases, screening to assess potential program eligibility using guided questions and rules, and applications for benefits. Notes that applications require detailed personal and household information and can be submitted in person, by phone, or online.

children.title: Determining a Client's Needs using Triage
children.summary: This section explains how workers use triage at intake to quickly assess a client’s immediate needs and suggest suitable services or programs. It also outlines related intake paths: identifying whether the client is existing, new, or anonymous; capturing and handling client life events, especially when there is or isn’t an active case; screening to determine potential program eligibility using high-level questions and eligibility rules; and starting benefit applications, including required evidence and submission channels.

children.title: Identifying Client Life Events and Dealing with them Effectively
children.summary: This section explains how CCI handles common intake workflows: identifying whether a client is existing, new, or anonymous; using triage to quickly assess immediate needs; managing client life events such as birth, marriage, or employment changes, with different handling for active versus inactive cases; screening clients for potential program eligibility using configurable questions and eligibility rules; and processing benefit applications with required evidence collected in person, by phone, or online.

children.title: Screening Clients to Determine Potential Eligibility for Programs
children.summary: Explains how CCI supports screening to assess a client’s potential eligibility for one or more programs using guided high-level questions and short eligibility rules built on IEG. Screening helps determine whether to proceed with a benefits application. The section sits within a broader intake flow that also covers identifying existing/new/anonymous clients, triage for immediate needs, life-event handling, referrals, and application submission channels (in person, by phone, or online).

children.title: Processing Client Applications for Programs
children.summary: This section explains how clients apply for program benefits after screening. It covers the need to submit an application for most programs, the types of required information captured as evidence (personal details, household composition, income, expenses, employment, education, etc.), and the main application channels: in-person at the agency, by phone, or online through a citizen portal or external system.

children.title: Referring a Client for Services
children.summary: Explains that a referral is an email sent to an external provider or agency to notify them a client needs services or benefits. Workers can create referrals at any stage of intake, either based on their own assessment during application/screening or from system-recommended services triggered by triage or life events. Notes that referrals can be made to external agencies and service providers, and points to a later section for more details.

Explains how CCI helps workers address a client’s needs through optional intake paths: identify whether the client is existing, new, or anonymous; use triage to quickly assess immediate needs; capture life events; screen for potential program eligibility with guided questions and eligibility rules; create referrals; or proceed to benefit applications. Notes that clients often seek a specific program and may be guided toward more suitable services. Also lists client need categories (food, shelter, safety, health, finance, education) and key application channels and required evidence.

Overview of Common Intake (CCI), a configurable agency-wide intake model for social service programs. It contrasts needs-based intake (triage, screening, application, benefit authorization, referrals, life events) with protection-based intake (reports, investigation, safety/risk, legal actions), and notes CCI mainly supports needs-based workflows while enabling external referrals. It explains the “No Wrong Door” approach, optional intake paths, PCR-based differential response, client need categories, client identification, triage, life events, screening, and application channels/evidence.

children.title: 1.3 Identifying the Client
Introduction
children.summary: Introduction to client identification and referral handling in CCI 8.1.2. Describes how intake workers process applications, including manual/system-driven steps, and how they can create referrals to external agencies or services at any stage. Explains the intake flow for identifying clients: searching with minimal criteria, matching against existing persons/prospects, quick registration with limited data, anonymous intake, and later full registration. Also notes that registration creates a person or prospect person participant role.

children.title: Searching for a Client
children.summary: Explains how intake workers identify clients in Cúram CCI. Covers initial search using minimal criteria across registered persons and prospect persons, reviewing matches to decide if the client is already in the system, and registering the client as either a prospect or fully registered client. Notes that intake may begin with minimal data or anonymously, and that registration creates a person or prospect person participant role. Also briefly mentions referrals to external services during intake.

children.title: Registering a Client
Starting Intake for an Anonymous Person
children.summary: Explains CCI intake and registration options for unknown clients: starting intake anonymously creates a prospect person record and unique identifier for triage/screening without full identity details. Covers quick registration of prospect persons with minimal data, later conversion to full person participants when more information is available, and merging duplicate prospect and registered person records through search and clearance. Also notes person registration validates required information and prevents duplicates, and that person/prospect data can be manually maintained after registration.

children.title: Registering a Prospect Person
Merging a Prospect Person with a Registered Person Record
children.summary: Explains that if both a prospect person record and a registered participant/person record exist for the same client, the prospect record can be merged into the participant record through the search and clearance process. This section sits within the broader intake/registration flow that distinguishes anonymous starts, quick registration of prospect persons, full person registration, duplicate prevention, and later maintenance of person/prospect person data.

This section explains how a client can be registered first as a prospect person when only minimal information is available, including via Quick Registration or anonymous intake. It notes that prospect persons can later be upgraded to full person participants once more information is collected. It also states that if a prospect record and a registered person record exist for the same client, they can be merged through search and clearance.

children.title: Registering a Person
children.summary: Explains person registration in Common Intake: it validates required information and checks for existing participants to avoid duplicate records. Also contrasts registering anonymous persons (creating a prospect person for triage/screening), prospect person quick registration with minimal data, and merging prospect records into existing participant records via search and clearance. Notes that person and prospect person information can be updated after registration, such as adding addresses.

Explains client registration in CCI as the step that assigns a participant role and creates either a person or prospect person record. It notes the worker can search for an existing client, register a new client with minimal or full information, or start intake anonymously. Registration supports later full registration, and person search spans both persons and prospect persons. This node is useful for understanding the basic registration outcome and how clients enter the system.

children.title: Maintaining Person and Prospect Person Information
children.summary: This section explains how intake workers manage person and prospect person records in CCI: starting intake for anonymous clients, creating prospect person records with minimal information, upgrading prospect persons to person participants, merging duplicate prospect/person records, and registering persons once required information is complete. It also notes that registration validates required data and checks for existing participants to prevent duplicates. Finally, it covers post-registration maintenance of person and prospect person details, such as updating addresses and other information manually.

children.title: Citizen Account
children.summary: Describes the Citizen Account feature in Cúram Common Intake. It lets clients securely apply for programs and view/manage home page data such as applications, screenings, triages, life events, referrals, activities, and contact details. Internal workers can create and link a Citizen Account on behalf of a client, but only if no account already exists and only when Universal Access is licensed. Also notes that account creation is handled through a wizard for entering login credentials.

children.title: Citizen Account
children.summary: Citizen Account describes a secure client account in Cúram Common Intake that lets clients apply for programs and access their home page, applications, screenings, triages, life events, referrals, activities, and contact information. It can also be created by an internal worker on behalf of a client and linked to the client’s home page, but only if no account already exists. Creation uses a login-credential wizard and is available only with a Universal Access license.

Explains how intake workers identify and register clients in CCI. Covers searching with minimal criteria across persons and prospect persons, deciding whether a client already exists, quick registration with limited data, anonymous intake, and later full registration. Describes creation of person or prospect person participant roles, duplicate checks, validation of required information, and merging prospect person records with existing participant records. Also notes that person/prospect person details can be maintained after registration, and that referrals to external agencies or services can be created at any stage of intake.

children.title: 1.4 Triaging a Client and Using the Results to Address Client Needs
Introduction
children.summary: This introduction explains automatically maintained CCI information and the Citizen Account feature. It lists shared participant data such as triages, screenings, applications, interviews, referrals, life events, and notes. It describes how internal workers can create and link a Citizen Account for a client, with login credentials entered through a wizard, and notes that this is available only with Universal Access licensing. It then begins section 1.4 on triaging clients and using results to address basic needs.

children.title: Running a Triage
children.summary: Explains how to run a triage in CCI: a worker asks a short set of multiple-choice questions at intake to quickly identify a client’s needs and generate recommended services, providers, or programs. Triage can be done for anonymous, prospect, or registered persons, cannot be saved mid-way, and requires at least one answer. Results may include urgent alerts and support referrals, benefit applications, eligibility screening, or emergency response. A history of completed triages is retained.

children.title: Triage Results
Alert Text
children.summary: Explains triage alert text in CCI: based on client answers, the system may display one or more alerts on the triage results tab to warn the worker about important or emergency information, such as calling emergency services immediately. Alerts are defined in the triage rule set and shown as part of the triage results, alongside recommended services and programs.

children.title: Mapping Services to Providers
Viewing Provider Details
children.summary: This section explains how workers view community service provider details in CCI. Providers are shown on a map based on agency and client location, with a search option for specific areas like “Springfield.” Selecting a provider opens detailed information to help choose the best match for client needs. The surrounding text also introduces referrals to community service providers and government programs, including prepopulated referral forms and send/edit/delete actions for unsent referrals.

CCI shows recommended community service providers on a map for clients’ basic needs, filtered by agency and client location, with search by area (e.g., Springfield). Workers can open a provider to view details and choose the best match. The section also introduces referrals to community service providers: prepopulated forms based on triage results, editable name/contact fields, and options to send, edit, or delete unsent referrals by email.

children.title: Referring Clients to Community Services
children.summary: This section explains how CCI helps workers refer clients to community services after triage. It covers mapping recommended providers by agency and client location, searching by area, and viewing provider details. It describes creating community service referrals from triage results using a pre-populated form with editable name fields, and notes that unsent referrals can be sent, edited, or deleted; sending is via email. It also briefly introduces recommended government programs and external agency referrals.

children.title: Recommending Government Programs to Clients
children.summary: Describes how CCI recommends government programs from triage results. The system identifies internal programs offered by the agency and external programs from other agencies based on client answers and CER rules. Workers can screen, apply for, or create referrals to internal programs; for external programs, they can screen or create an email-based referral via a 2-step wizard. Referrals are pre-populated with program and client contact details and can be edited, sent, or deleted before sending.

children.title: Providing Context Sensitive Help To Case Workers
children.summary: This section explains the advisor feature that gives case workers context-sensitive tips during intake, especially from triage data, and can link to relevant programs such as food or cash assistance. It also notes that triage-related notes can be captured and maintained from the triage results tab. The section then introduces screening clients for potential program eligibility: guided questions, agency-configured eligibility rules, recommended programs, urgent alerts, and screening history.

children.title: Triage Notes
children.summary: This section explains triage notes and related guidance in the intake workflow. It says workers can create and maintain notes tied specifically to a triage record, accessed from the triage results tab, with more detail referenced in Case Worker Tools. It also introduces context-sensitive advisor help during triage, which suggests next steps based on entered data, such as screening for food or cash assistance. The page then begins the screening-for-eligibility chapter, describing agency-configured screening rules, recommendations, alerts, and screening history.

Triage Results shows what CCI displays after triage answers are submitted: recommended community services, service providers, government programs, and urgent alerts derived from CER rules. It explains actions workers can take from results, including screening, applying for benefits, creating referrals, and responding to emergencies. It also covers provider maps and details, referrals to community services and external agencies via prepopulated forms/wizards, and the triage history record that stores date/time, alerts, and recommendations.

Explains how CCI triages clients to identify basic needs and recommend services, providers, and programs. Triage can be run for anonymous, prospect, or registered persons using short multiple-choice questions, cannot be saved mid-way, and requires at least one answer. Results may show urgent alerts, provider maps/details, referrals, and options to screen, apply for benefits, or refer to internal or external programs. A completed triage history is retained with date/time, alerts, and recommendations.

children.title: 1.5 Screening Clients to Determine Potential Eligibility for Programs
Introduction
children.summary: This introduction explains screening clients for potential program eligibility in CCI. It covers guided, agency-configurable screening for anonymous, prospect, or registered people; eligibility rules that generate recommended government programs and urgent alerts; and next-step actions such as applying for programs, creating external referrals, or responding to emergencies. It also notes that screening history is stored with recommendations, alerts, and timestamps. The section emphasizes that CCI provides the infrastructure, but agencies configure their own screening scripts and rules.

children.title: Screening a Client for Potential Eligibility
Screening for a Specific Program or a Range of Programs
children.summary: Explains how CCI handles screening for one or multiple programs. Agencies can configure single or multiple screening categories; if multiple are enabled, the worker selects a category first, otherwise they go straight to a new screening and choose programs. The section describes guided, configurable screening questions and business rules used to determine potential eligibility, notes that results are not a guarantee, and covers saving, discarding, or resuming in-progress screenings while preventing duplicates. It also mentions configurable evidence types and IEG-based question grouping.

children.title: Programs that Clients can be Screened For
children.summary: This section explains that agencies can configure which programs clients may be screened for, with each program having its own business rules to assess potential eligibility. It notes that screening uses gathered client data and that multiple or single screening categories can be configured, with guided questions and evidence capture supporting the process. The section helps determine which programs are available for screening and how eligibility is evaluated.

children.title: Capturing Client Evidence for a Screening
children.summary: This section explains how a screening captures client evidence through configurable, sequential question groups. Agencies can define many evidence types and organize questions by topic such as household, benefits, income, resources, and expenses. A screening can be submitted once minimum required information is entered. It also notes that CCI uses the Intelligent Evidence Gathering (IEG) infrastructure to configure the guided screening script for available programs.

Explains how CCI lets workers screen anonymous, prospect, or registered clients for one or more agency-configured programs using guided questions and eligibility rules. Coverage includes starting screening from triage or directly, configuring single vs. multiple screening categories, saving/discarding/resuming in-progress screenings, and preventing duplicates. It also describes configurable program-specific business rules, evidence capture via sequential question groups, and IEG-based question grouping. Screening results indicate potential eligibility only, not guaranteed eligibility.

children.title: Recommending Programs for Clients based on Screening Results
Applying for Programs based on Screening Results
children.summary: Explains how intake workers use screening results to recommend programs. After triage, Cúram Eligibility Rule Set results show potential eligibility, short determinations, and urgent alerts. Workers can then start applications for agency programs or create referrals to external agencies via a 2-step wizard. It also notes that screening may return programs with undetermined eligibility, can display multiple alerts, and allows notes tied to the screening record.

children.title: Alert Text
children.summary: Explains that screening results may display urgent alert text to highlight critical client information after triage answers are processed by the eligibility rules. Alerts are based on the client’s responses, can be multiple, and may instruct the worker to take immediate action, such as calling emergency services. Useful as the node describing how screening alerts inform workers during program eligibility screening.

children.title: Screening Notes
children.summary: This section notes that CCI lets workers capture and maintain notes tied specifically to a screening record. It is a brief pointer to note-taking functionality within screening, with a reference to Case Worker Tools for more detail.

Explains how screening results guide program recommendations after triage. The CER displays potential eligibility, short determinations, and urgent alerts for selected programs, including cases where eligibility cannot be determined. Workers can then start agency program applications, create external agency referrals via a 2-step wizard, respond to emergencies, and add notes tied to the screening record.

This section explains CCI screening for potential program eligibility. It covers screening anonymous, prospect, or registered clients from triage or directly; using agency-configured single or multiple screening categories; and guiding workers through IEG-based question groups and program-specific business rules. Screening results show only potential eligibility, not a guarantee, and may be saved, resumed, or discarded while avoiding duplicate in-progress records. It also notes recommended programs, urgent alerts, and next actions such as starting applications, making external referrals, or responding to emergencies.

children.title: 1.6 Lifecycle of a Client's Application for Benefits: From Creation to Disposition
Introduction
children.summary: This section explains how screening results drive intake actions. After a worker submits triage answers, the system shows program eligibility results, urgent alerts, and short determinations using CER rules. Based on screening, the worker can start applications for agency programs or create referrals to external agencies via a 2-step wizard. It also covers alert text for emergencies and notes that screening-specific notes can be recorded and maintained.

children.title: Starting an Application
Adding an Application to an Existing Case
children.summary: Explains how an intake worker can add a new application to an existing case for an existing client, making the application part of the existing integrated case so new evidence is added to that case. Context also covers application submission workflow, where intake and eligibility workers may share responsibilities, and notes that applications start with a primary client and may include additional clients. It mentions guided question flows for capturing application information and merging application/interview steps for some programs.

Explains how an application is started in CCI from triage/life event/screening results, a client’s application list, or the advisor, with optional selection of application and program types. Describes that applications begin for one primary client and may include additional clients, but the claimant cannot be changed after start. Also covers adding a new application to an existing case so evidence goes into the existing integrated case, and outlines guided question flows for capturing application data, including merged application/interview steps for some programs.

children.title: Capturing Application Information
Capturing Client Evidence
children.summary: This section explains how client evidence is captured during application intake in Cúram CCI. It notes that an application can be submitted once minimum required information is entered, and for programs requiring an intake interview, the application and interview steps are merged so details like income and expenses are captured together. It also covers who handles intake and eligibility, where applications can be started, adding applications to existing cases, and how guided questions/IEG flows collect application information.

This section explains how Cúram CCI captures application data during intake. It covers starting an application from triage/screening results, client application lists, or the advisor; adding an application to an existing case; and using guided questions/IEG flows to collect required information. It also notes that intake and eligibility workers share processing responsibilities, applications are for a primary client but may include additional clients, and an application can be submitted once minimum required information is entered. For programs with intake interviews, interview and application steps may be merged to capture evidence together.

children.title: Saving and Resuming or Discarding an In Progress Application
children.summary: Explains that an intake worker can stop an in-progress application at any point, either saving it for later resumption or discarding it entirely based on the client’s wishes. It then introduces submission as the step that officially registers the application with the agency. Also notes common submission channels (in person, phone, online, by post) and that submission may require the client to accept rights/responsibilities and confirm accuracy.

children.title: Submitting An Application
Submitting an Application in Person or by Phone
children.summary: Explains how an application is formally submitted and what channels CCI supports. Covers in-person/phone submissions, where the intake worker can submit once mandatory information is captured; online submissions routed to CCI and possibly auto-processed or sent to a worker; and postal submissions routed through the post room or scanning/document systems. Also notes that clients may need to accept rights/responsibilities and certify accuracy, and that in-progress applications can be saved, resumed, or discarded.

children.title: Submitting an Application Online
children.summary: This section explains how an application is submitted and what happens after submission. It covers saving, resuming, or discarding an in-progress application, the need to submit to create an official agency record, and client confirmation of rights/responsibilities and truthfulness. It lists supported channels: in person, by phone, online via a citizen portal, and by post. It also notes that online applications may be routed automatically or to a worker/work queue, depending on program rules and whether additional review is needed.

children.title: Submitting an Application by Post
children.summary: Describes how paper applications submitted by post are handled. The agency post room routes the application to the appropriate intake worker or work queue, sometimes via scanning/document management systems. The intake worker reviews the information entered, notes that paper applications often have less detail than online ones, and determines next steps. The section sits within broader application submission guidance, including saving/resuming, discarding in-progress applications, and submitting applications through other channels.

children.title: Routing a Submitted Application
children.summary: Explains what happens after an application is submitted: how it is routed to a work queue, specific user, or group based on routing policy; default online submissions go to the online applications work queue, while in-agency submissions are assigned to the submitting worker. Also covers recording application data, including evidence, filing date, receipt method, programs applied for, and client/household member details, plus automatic vs manual application date handling.

children.title: Recording Application Information
Recording Application Details and Application Date
children.summary: Explains how application details are recorded when an application is submitted, including filing date, method of receipt, and programs applied for. Describes automatic application date rules for online submissions (today during business hours, next business date otherwise) and manual date entry/editing for in-agency applications. Also notes routing behavior after submission and that the system stores client and evidence information on the case, with a read-only copy retained for records.

children.title: Recording Client Information
children.summary: Explains how client information from an application is recorded in Cúram. It notes that mandatory data must be available before submission; otherwise an interview may be scheduled to capture missing details. Once submitted, personal information such as address and date of birth is stored for the client, and any additional household members are also recorded as clients. The section sits alongside related topics on routing submitted applications and recording application details, dates, and evidence.

children.title: Recording Evidence
children.summary: Explains how submitted application evidence is recorded: captured evidence is mapped to evidence records and becomes available for workers to view and update. Also notes that on submission the system sets processing timers based on agency-configured time limits. The section situates evidence within broader post-submission application management, including viewing, updating, and resolving evidence issues as part of the application workflow.

Describes how in-agency applications are recorded when submitted: application details, client information, and evidence are stored on the case, with a read-only copy kept for records. Covers application filing date and receipt method, program associations, and automatic date rules for online submissions (today during business hours, next business date otherwise) versus manual/default date handling in-agency. Also notes that client personal details and additional household members are recorded as clients, and submitted applications may be routed to queues or users based on agency policy.

children.title: Processing the Application in a Timely Manner
children.summary: Explains how applications are processed after submission in CCI: processing timers are configured by the agency and start on submission to meet program deadlines (for example, 30 days for food assistance). Workers can then view, add, remove, and update application data such as clients, programs, interviews, evidence, eligibility checks, related cases, notes, attachments, and communications. It also describes the Application To Do list driven by CER rules, and the need to resolve prospect clients by matching them to existing persons or registering them as new.

Explains how an application is formally submitted and recorded in CCI. Covers saving, resuming, or discarding an in-progress application; client acceptance of rights/responsibilities and truthfulness; supported submission channels (in person, phone, online portal, post); and how submitted applications are routed to workers or queues. Also describes recording application details, evidence, filing date, receipt method, programs, and client/household information, including automatic date rules for online submissions and manual date setting for in-agency or postal applications.

children.title: Managing Application Information
children.summary: Describes what workers can do after an application is submitted: view, add, remove, and update captured information such as clients, programs, timers, interviews, evidence, eligibility checks, related cases, PCR, notes, attachments, and communications. Explains the Application To Do list, driven by CER rules, for outstanding processing tasks. Also covers searching and resolving prospect clients against existing persons, including household members, and notes that application timers are set on submission to meet processing deadlines.

children.title: Searching and Clearing the Clients on an Application
Searching for and Matching a Client
children.summary: Explains how an intake worker searches for and matches a prospect client to an existing registered person, merges details when a match is found, or registers the person if not found. Also covers adding additional clients later, including initial search, registration, and setting the date they’re considered included. Describes removing additional clients (not the claimant) by deleting household member evidence, and transferring an application to an existing case only after all prospects are matched or registered and existing integrated cases are reviewed.

children.title: Registering a Client as a Person Participant
children.summary: Explains how an intake worker handles client identity during application intake: searching for and matching prospects to registered persons, merging details when a match is found, or registering the prospect as a person participant if no match exists. Also covers adding additional clients later, including searching/registering them and setting an effective inclusion date, removing additional clients from an application (but not the claimant), and transferring an application to an existing case once all prospect persons are matched or registered.

children.title: Adding an Additional Client to an Application
children.summary: Explains how an intake worker manages clients on an application: searching and matching prospects to registered persons, registering unmatched prospects as persons, adding additional household members later with an effective inclusion date, removing extra clients (but not the claimant), and transferring the application to an existing case after all prospects are matched or registered. Useful for understanding client lifecycle actions on an application and when transfer to an existing case becomes available.

children.title: Removing an Additional Client from an Application
children.summary: Explains how an intake worker searches, matches, registers, adds, and removes clients on an application. It notes that additional household members can be added later if missing, with an effective date for when they count on the application. Clients may be removed if listed in error, but the claimant cannot be removed. Removing a household member requires deleting their evidence and closing the related task. Also introduces transferring an application to an existing case after all prospects are matched or registered.

Explains that all clients on an application must be registered before processing. Prospect persons (including household members) are searched and matched to existing registered persons or registered as new persons if no match is found. Notes that additional clients may be added or removed from an application, and that this section uses Cúram participant functionality for search, match, and registration. Also mentions application evidence, timers, and other captured information are available once the application is submitted.

children.title: Transferring an Application to an Existing Case
children.summary: Explains how an application can be transferred to an existing case when any applicant is already a registered person with integrated cases. The intake worker must review those existing cases and decide whether the new application should be linked. Transfer is only available after all prospect persons on the application are either matched to existing registered persons or fully registered. Once selected, the application is associated with the existing case.

children.title: Scheduling a Client Interview
children.summary: Explains how intake workers schedule client interviews to complete an application. Interviews may be required for some programs or only when information is missing/conflicting; they can occur immediately when the client is present or be scheduled later for remote/external applications. Captured details include attendees, date/time, and location. Workers can review interviews in the application calendar or personal calendar, and can invite, edit, complete, or cancel interviews. Includes brief transition into managing application evidence.

children.title: Managing Application Evidence
Working with Evidence in the Evidence Workspace
Maintaining Evidence
children.summary: Explains how intake workers maintain evidence on an application: view, add, modify, remove, discard in-edit evidence, then apply changes, optionally with supervisor approval or evidence transfer. Covers evidence verifications by evidence type and notes this requires the Verification Engine. Also describes cross-application/case evidence sharing via the Evidence Broker, including accepting or rejecting incoming evidence. Finally, outlines checking eligibility at any point after submission using current active and in-edit evidence and agency-specific rules.

children.title: Verifying Evidence
children.summary: Explains evidence verification management in Cúram: intake workers can view and manage verifications for a specific evidence type, including a list of all verifications on an application and verification details at the evidence type level. Notes this feature requires the Cúram Verification Engine module. This section sits within broader evidence maintenance and application/case evidence sharing workflows.

This section introduces the Evidence Workspace for managing application evidence after submission. It explains available views and actions for evidence on an application, including reviewing, adding, editing, removing, and handling in-edit evidence, with changes applied later. It also points to evidence verification support by type, requires the Verification Engine, and covers evidence transfer/sharing across applications or cases via the Evidence Broker, including accepting or rejecting incoming evidence. It notes eligibility can be checked anytime using current active and in-edit evidence and agency rules.

children.title: Managing Evidence across Applications and Cases
children.summary: Explains how CCI manages evidence across applications and cases through the Evidence Broker. Agencies can configure sharing of active evidence between case types; evidence from unrelated cases can appear in the Incoming Evidence list for intake workers to accept or reject. Also notes evidence workspace views, maintain/edit/apply evidence actions, optional supervisor approval and evidence transfer, verification management, and eligibility checking using active/in-edit evidence and program-specific rules.

Describes how caseworkers manage submitted application evidence in the Evidence Workspace. Covers available views and actions to review, add, edit, remove, and apply evidence; handling evidence that is in edit; and checking eligibility using current active and in-edit evidence plus agency rules. Also notes evidence verification support by type requires the Verification Engine, and evidence can be transferred or shared across applications/cases through the Evidence Broker, with incoming evidence accepted or rejected.

children.title: Checking Eligibility on the Application
children.summary: Explains the Check Eligibility function on an application. Intake workers can run eligibility checks any time after submission and after all prospect clients are registered. The check can be repeated and evaluates the current active and in-edit evidence on the application to indicate possible benefits. Eligibility is driven by program-specific rules configured by the agency through CCI infrastructure, such as using Income Support rules for food assistance applications.

children.title: Setting the Application Status to Ready For Determination
children.summary: Explains how an application is marked “Ready for Determination” after intake and the resulting routing options. By default, CCI sends the application to the ready-for-determination work queue for eligibility worker review, though agencies can keep it with the current user or route it to a specific user. Also notes the default behavior when an eligibility worker sets this status, plus brief related guidance on adding/withdrawing program requests and handling online withdrawal requests.

children.title: Managing Programs on an Application
Adding a Program
children.summary: Workers can add a new program request to an existing application by choosing the program type and requested date. Programs can be added even if they were not originally on the application or were previously withdrawn. Adding a program updates that program’s application timers based on the add date. This section sits within broader application management, including program withdrawal and online withdrawal handling.

children.title: Withdrawing a Program
Managing an Online Withdrawal Request
children.summary: Explains how online withdrawal requests are handled in CCI. A client-submitted withdrawal request is linked to the relevant application, and either an intake or eligibility worker can confirm it. Online withdrawal requests are also routed by default to a dedicated work queue for review and confirmation. This section sits within broader guidance on managing program requests, including adding and withdrawing programs on an application.

This section explains how workers can add or withdraw program requests on an application. A program can be withdrawn at the client’s request by entering a withdrawal date and reason. It also covers online withdrawal requests: these are linked to the relevant application, can be confirmed by intake or eligibility workers, and are routed by default to the online withdrawal request work queue for review.

children.title: Reopening a Program
children.summary: Explains how denied or withdrawn programs can be reopened, including eligible application states, configurable reopening reasons/comments, state changes back to Pending and Submitted, reopen history, and timer resumption options. Also covers determining recommended programs via eligibility checks, creating referrals when no eligible decisions exist, and PCR assessment rules for priority, complexity, and risk, including trigger scenarios and optional advice display.

This section explains how intake and eligibility workers manage program requests on an application. They can add new or previously withdrawn programs by choosing the program type and requested date, which resets that program’s timers based on the add date. They can also withdraw a program by entering a withdrawal date and reason. Online withdrawal requests are linked to the application, routed by default to the online withdrawal request work queue, and can be confirmed by workers.

children.title: Determining Recommended Programs
Creating Referrals
children.summary: Explains that if an application has no eligible program decisions, the eligibility worker may create a referral to another agency or community service for additional help. Also notes related application-processing topics on the page: reopening denied/withdrawn programs, determining recommended programs, and configuring PCR (priority, complexity, risk) assessments triggered by various application events.

Explains how the eligibility worker determines recommended programs by reviewing or rerunning eligibility checks on application data. If at least one eligible decision exists, the worker continues processing and authorizing benefits. If none exist, the worker may create a referral to another agency or community service for additional help. The page also notes nearby application-process topics, including reopening denied or withdrawn programs and triggering/configuring PCR (priority, complexity, risk) assessments during various application events.

children.title: Assessing Priority, Complexity, and Risk for an Application
children.summary: This section explains how an application’s priority, complexity, and risk (PCR) are assessed during its lifecycle. It lists configurable trigger points for PCR assessment: application submission, prospect matching/registration, evidence application, eligibility checks, adding/removing program requests, or manual worker action. It also notes that the agency can display advice based on PCR classifications after assessment.

children.title: Managing Contact with the Agency
children.summary: This section explains how workers manage ongoing contact on an application: adding and maintaining notes, attaching supporting documents, and recording communications such as calls, emails, and letters. It notes that attachments can include common file types and are accessible to authorized users. It also covers application processing timeframes and timer tracking for reporting and compliance. Finally, it describes authorizing or declining benefits from eligibility results and the configurable event triggered when benefits are authorized.

children.title: Managing Timeframes
children.summary: This section explains that most programs impose application processing deadlines and that intake/eligibility workers must process cases within application timers, mainly for reporting and supervisor monitoring; agencies may be penalized for poor timeliness. It notes that CCI timers use Cúram Milestones functionality.

children.title: Authorizing and Declining Benefits
Appealing a Program Denial
children.summary: Explains how users appeal denied program applications: a single appeal can cover one or more denied programs in the same application, or be added to an existing appeal case. Filing an appeal creates an appeal case that drives statements, hearings, decision review, and implementation. The section also describes where appeals are viewed and notes three appeal types: hearing case, hearing review, and judicial review, configured in Administration. It briefly outlines case establishment options for applications and how integrated cases are created and associated.

Explains how an eligibility worker authorizes or declines benefits by reviewing eligibility results. The authorize/decline actions can apply to each eligibility decision or assistance unit, depending on agency configuration. When benefits are authorized, CCI emits an event that agencies can use to customize downstream processing, such as creating a product delivery or a service plan for the approved benefit.

children.title: Establishing a Case for an Application
Creating a New Case
children.summary: Explains how an application can establish a new integrated case when submitted, depending on agency configuration. Evidence and clients from the application are added to the new case(s), and the case may become visible to the user at different stages, such as after a program is approved. Also notes the broader options for case establishment: create a new case, add to an existing case, or transfer to an existing case.

Describes how intake workers establish a case for an application. Agencies can configure three options: create a new case, add the application to an existing case, or transfer it to an existing case when clients already exist as registered persons. Processing is done through the application, not the underlying case. For new cases, submitting the application can create one or more integrated cases that receive all captured evidence and clients, and the case may become visible later in the process, such as after a program is approved.

children.title: Disposing the Application
Notifying the Client of the Eligibility Determination
children.summary: This section explains that client notification about an eligibility determination can be sent only after an application’s status is updated to disposed, and that this is agency-configurable. It also covers application ownership: who can move an application through its lifecycle, how ownership can be assigned to users, units, positions, or work queues, and that owners handle processing, eligibility checks, final decisions, and benefit authorization. It begins an overview of application status transitions, including In Progress, Submitting, Submit Failed, and Submitted.

Explains when an application is automatically set to disposed: once all associated program applications are approved, denied, or withdrawn. If programs are approved, ongoing eligibility is handled on the integrated case. Client eligibility notifications may be sent only after disposal and can be agency-configured. Also covers application ownership: owner privileges, assignment to users/units/positions/work queues, and owner responsibilities for processing, eligibility, final decisions, and benefit authorization. Introduces application status transitions: In Progress, Submitting, Submit Failed, and Submitted.

children.title: Application Ownership
children.summary: Explains application ownership and lifecycle. Applications are moved by users with application owner privileges; the owner handles processing, eligibility checks, final determinations, and benefit authorization. Ownership can be assigned to a user, organization unit, position, or work queue, and may be manually changed with event hooks for validation. Also outlines key application statuses: In Progress, Submitting, Submit Failed, and Submitted, including when transitions occur. Mentions applications become disposed once all associated program applications are approved, denied, or withdrawn.

children.title: Application Statuses
children.summary: Explains application lifecycle statuses and ownership. Covers when an application becomes disposed (all associated program applications are approved, denied, or withdrawn), client notification after disposal, and how application ownership can be assigned to users, organization units, positions, or work queues. Includes the main status transitions: In Progress, Submitting, Submit Failed, and Submitted, with rules for how applications move between them, including submission failure and reopening a program on Ready for Determination or Disposed applications.

Covers the full lifecycle of a benefits application in CCI: starting from screening/life event/results or client lists, capturing and saving in-progress data, submitting via in person/phone/online/post, and routing. Explains adding to or transferring into existing cases, registering prospect clients, scheduling interviews, managing evidence, eligibility checks, PCR assessments, notes/attachments/communications, timers, authorizing/declining benefits, appeals, and when applications become disposed. Also defines ownership roles and application status transitions (In Progress, Submitting, Submit Failed, Submitted).

children.title: 1.7 Helping a Client Deal with a Life Event
Introduction
children.summary: Introduces CCI life event functionality for registered persons only, not prospects. Explains that life events are client changes (e.g. marriage, birth, job change) that may affect benefits and must be reported. Describes how workers create life events from the list or advisor, answer guided questions, and transmit updated evidence via the evidence broker to configured active cases for case owners to apply. Notes recommendations may be shown based on responses. Also states CCI has no preconfigured scripts/rules, but supports configurable life event scripts and rules.

children.title: Recording a Client's Life Event
Recording a Life Event for a Client with an Active Case
children.summary: Explains how to record a life event for a client with an active case in CCI. The worker uses guided questions to capture the event and any related changes, then the details are transmitted via the evidence broker to relevant case owners, who decide whether to apply them to the active case. The section also notes that recommendations may be shown, such as referrals to community services or additional benefits. Life events apply only to registered persons and differ from the triage process used when no active case exists.

children.title: Recording a Life Event for a Client that does not have an Active Case
children.summary: This section explains how CCI handles recording a life event for a client without an active case. It covers what a life event is, notes that life event functionality applies only to registered persons (not prospects), and describes the general workflow: a worker selects a life event, answers guided questions, and the system may provide recommendations. It also contrasts this with the active-case flow, where captured evidence is transmitted to relevant case owners through the evidence broker.

Explains CCI life event recording for registered clients. A worker can start a life event from a list or advisor, then answer guided questions to capture the event and related changes. If the client has active cases, the details are sent via the evidence broker to relevant case owners, who decide whether to apply updates. The system may also show recommendations for referrals or benefits. Life events are not available for prospects and differ from triage used when no active case exists.

children.title: Recommending Programs and Services based on the Results of a Life Event
Alert Text
children.summary: This section explains configurable alert text shown on life event results pages. Alerts are based on the life event type and may warn the worker about important impacts, such as pregnancy affecting active cases, eligibility, or benefit entitlement. It notes that evidence changes for the pregnant person can be broadcast to relevant cases. CCI lets agencies configure which alerts appear to workers for each life event type.

children.title: Services Map
children.summary: This section explains the Services Map in CCI/Cúram life event results. Agencies can configure which community services and providers appear on the map based on a client’s life event answers. Recommended services/providers are displayed, with preselected services shown initially. It also points to related map functionality and notes that workers can use these recommendations to create referrals when appropriate.

children.title: Referrals
children.summary: Explains how, after a life event assessment, the system recommends programs, services, and providers, shows alerts, and lets the worker create referrals, screen for eligibility, apply for benefits, or respond to emergencies. It also notes that completed life events retain a history of answers, alerts, and recommendations. The referrals part specifically says workers can refer clients to community service providers when agency programs are unavailable or the client is ineligible.

children.title: Recommended Programs
children.summary: This section explains how the system recommends government programs after a life event and how workers can screen, apply, or create referrals based on client answers. It covers internal vs. external programs, including screening/applying for internal programs and creating or sending referrals to external agencies. It also introduces life event notes and defines referrals, showing where referrals can be created in the workflow (referrals list, screening, triage, and life event results).

children.title: Life Event Notes
children.summary: This section explains that life event records support capturing and maintaining notes tied specifically to a life event, accessible from the Life Event Results tab. It also situates life events within recommended programs and referral workflows, noting that workers can screen, apply for, or create referrals to internal or external programs based on client answers and system rules. External referrals use a 2-step wizard, can be edited or sent later, and are emailed to providers.

Explains how life event answers drive CER-based results that recommend relevant government programs, services, and providers. The results page may show configurable alerts, a services map with preselected/recommended services, and options to create referrals, screen/apply for benefits, or handle emergencies. It also notes that completed life events keep a history of answers, date/time, alerts, and recommendations. Includes guidance on referring clients to community service providers when agency programs are unavailable or the client is ineligible.

Explains CCI’s life event workflow for registered clients, not prospects. Workers select or launch a life event, answer guided or triage-style questions, and send updated evidence via the evidence broker to configured active cases for case owners to apply. For clients without active cases, answers drive recommendations for benefits, referrals, or emergency response. The section also covers configurable alerts, services maps, CER-based recommendations, and the history stored for completed life events.

children.title: 1.8 Referring a Client to External Agencies and Service Providers
What is a Referral?
children.summary: A referral is a document sent to an external provider or agency to notify them that a client needs services or benefits. This section explains that intake or eligibility workers can create referrals at any point in intake, most often after triage, screening, application, or life events. Referrals can be created from the referrals list/client home page, screening results, triage results, or life events results, based on worker knowledge or system recommendations.

children.title: Creating a Referral
Selecting a Service Provider
children.summary: Explains how workers create referrals in CCI by selecting community service providers on a map, from a referrals list, or by searching for an external agency. The form is prefilled with provider details, but the client’s first and last name/contact details must be entered before saving. Referrals are stored with a creation date, can be viewed, edited, deleted, or sent from a person’s open referrals list, and are emailed if an address exists; otherwise they remain unsent for printing or mailing.

children.title: Selecting an External Agency
children.summary: This section explains how a worker creates referrals to external agencies when benefits or programs are not available through the agency. The worker searches for and selects an agency, enters the client’s contact details, and saves the referral; the creation date is stored. It also notes that workers can view a person’s open community service and external agency referrals, edit/delete/send them, create new referrals from the list, and that referrals are sent by email when an address exists, otherwise remain unsent for printing or mailing.

children.title: Viewing a List of Open Referrals
children.summary: This section explains how workers view a person’s open referrals list. It covers that the list includes all community service and external agency referrals linked to the person, shows referral details for each record, and lets the worker edit, delete, send, or create a new referral from the list.

This section defines a referral as a document sent to an external provider or agency to inform them a client needs services or benefits. It explains that workers can create referrals at several points in CCI, including the referrals list/client home page, screening results, triage results, and life event results. It also notes that referrals may be created from system recommendations or worker knowledge, and that external program referrals can be sent by email, or left unsent for later editing, deletion, or sending.

children.title: What Happens after a Referral is Created?
children.summary: After a referral is created, CCI sends community service or external agency referrals to the provider/agency if an email address is on file. If no email exists, the referral remains unsent, and the worker can print it for the client to deliver or send it by post. This section follows referral creation and briefly explains the post-creation handling process.

This section explains what a referral is and when intake or eligibility workers can create one for external agencies or service providers. It covers referrals made during intake, especially after triage, screening, application, or life events, and notes they can be created from the referrals list/client home page, screening results, triage results, or life events results. It also mentions referrals may be based on worker knowledge or system recommendations, and that external program referrals can be sent by email or left unsent for later editing, deletion, or sending.

children.title: 1.9 Case Worker Tools
Case Worker Tools
children.summary: This section introduces case worker tools available throughout the intake process: advisor, notes, PCR, alerts, and user home page tools. It sits after referral workflows, which cover selecting community service providers or external agencies, creating and sending referrals, viewing open referrals, and handling unsent referrals when no email address is available.

children.title: The Advisor
children.summary: This section describes the Advisor, a CER Engine–based caseworker tool that gives context-sensitive tips, reminders, and clickable advice during intake and evidence capture. It can guide next steps, flag missing mandatory data, and be agency-configured. It also covers note taking throughout the intake process and explains Priority, Complexity, Risk (PCR) as an agency-defined way to tailor responses based on urgency, situation complexity, and risk.

children.title: Notes
children.summary: This section explains CCI’s in-process support features: the Advisor and Notes, plus Priority, Complexity, Risk (PCR). The Advisor is a CER-based tool that gives context-sensitive tips, reminders, and next-step guidance during intake and evidence capture, with agency-configurable rules and text. Notes can be entered at many points in the process to record client details and maintain history. PCR is defined as priority, complexity, and risk, and agencies can configure PCR rule sets and responses for triage, screening, life events, and application events.

children.title: Priority, Complexity, Risk
children.summary: This section explains Priority, Complexity, Risk (PCR) as an agency-configurable mechanism for differential response to clients. It defines priority as time to handle a case, complexity as the situation’s difficulty, and risk as potential impact to the agency. It also notes that the CER Engine supports agency-defined PCR rules and responses, and that triage, screening, and life events are inherently PCR assessments, with hooks for PCR rules at key application events.

children.title: Alerts
children.summary: Explains how alerts are generated from triage, screening, and life event rule sets to flag important client information, including urgent situations like emergency needs. Also covers user home page tools: searching for clients, starting anonymous intake, viewing assigned applications, and monitoring work queues for online applications and applications awaiting determination. Includes summary views for intake worker supervisors to redirect overdue or soon-due tasks.

children.title: User Home Page Tools
Find Client
children.summary: Describes the Find Client function on the user home page. Workers can search for a client using basic criteria, with a single match opening the person home page and multiple matches showing a list. It also lets workers start an intake for an anonymous person; once registered as a prospect person, a unique reference number is created and intake can begin. This section sits within user home page tools, alongside application and work queue summaries.

children.title: Assigned Applications
children.summary: Shows how intake and eligibility workers view assigned applications on the home page as a due-date summary, helping identify overdue or soon-due cases for processing. This section sits within user home page tools alongside client search, anonymous intake, and work queue summaries for online applications, applications awaiting determination, and supervisor task redirection.

children.title: Online Applications Work Queue
children.summary: This section explains the online applications work queue. When a client submits an online application, a task is created for intake workers to review. Workers can see summary information, identify overdue or soon-due tasks, open the task list, and reserve tasks from the queue. It also notes a supervisor view that summarizes tasks by processing deadline and supports redirecting tasks to the most suitable worker.

children.title: Applications Awaiting Determination Work Queue
children.summary: Explains the Applications Awaiting Determination work queue: once an intake worker marks an application “ready for determination,” it moves into this queue. Eligibility workers can view task summaries, see overdue or soon-due items, open a task list, and reserve tasks from the queue. This section also sits among related user home page tools and work queues for intake and supervisory routing.

children.title: Online Applications Work Queue for Supervisor
children.summary: Explains the supervisor view of the Online Applications Work Queue. Supervisors can see a summary of queue tasks by processing deadline, quickly identify overdue or soon-due tasks, and redirect them to the most suitably equipped worker. This section is useful for understanding supervisor oversight and task reassignment within the online application intake process.

User Home Page Tools lets workers search for a client from the home page, start intake for an anonymous person, and view application/work queue summaries. Find Client supports basic search, opens a single match directly, lists multiple matches, and creates a prospect reference number before intake begins. Assigned Applications shows due-date summaries for intake and eligibility workers. Online Applications and Applications Awaiting Determination queues provide summary, overdue/due-soon views, task lists, and reservation. Supervisors can review online application deadlines and redirect tasks to suitable workers.
