Case Worker Tools describes intake support features: the CER Engine–based Advisor gives context-sensitive tips, next-step guidance, and reminders, including for missing mandatory data; Notes can be captured at many points and maintain history; PCR lets agencies define priority, complexity, and risk rules for differential response. It also covers alerts and user home page tools for finding clients, starting anonymous intake, and monitoring assigned, online, and overdue applications/work queues, including supervisor redirection.

children.title: 1.10 Defining The Intake Process
Introduction
children.summary: This introduction explains that Common Intake (CCI) is highly configurable to fit an agency’s intake process, supporting triage, screening, application, life event, referral, and PCR processing, with functions assignable by user role. It introduces triage via IEG scripts and CER rules, which produce recommended services and alerts. It also describes the provider map used to display recommended community services, including configurable preselection, location boundaries, center point, and zoom level.

children.title: Defining Triage
The Provider Map
children.summary: This section explains the Provider Map used after triage or screening to show recommended community service providers near the client. It covers configurable preselection limits for returned services, agency- and client-location-based map filtering, optional map centering, geographic boundaries for which providers appear, and adjustable zoom level affecting visibility of provider icons. Useful for understanding how CCI presents and narrows provider recommendations on the results page.

children.title: Configuring the display of Google Maps for internal triage
Generating an API Key for Google Maps
children.summary: Explains how to enable Google Maps geocoding and triage map display in Cúram. Set `curam.miscapp.geocode.enabled` to true to store and retrieve latitude/longitude for participant addresses, then use either the standard Google Maps API or Google Maps for Business. For the standard API, store the key in `curam.miscapp.geocode.googlemapsapikey`; for Business, use client ID and signing key properties. Includes a note that enabling geocoding uses Google Geocoding API V3 and implies acceptance of Google Maps Terms, plus a brief guide to generating a Google Maps API key.

children.title: Setting an API key for Google Maps
children.summary: Explains how to obtain a Google Maps API key from Google, then configure it in Cúram by logging in as sysadmin and updating the application property `curam.miscapp.geocode.googlemapsapikey` via Property Administration. Also notes enabling and publishing the geo-code enabled property to use Google Geocoding API V3 for retrieving latitude and longitude from addresses.

children.title: Enabling the retrieval of the latitude and longitude of addresses
children.summary: This section explains how to enable geocoding in Cúram so Google Maps can retrieve latitude and longitude for addresses. It covers obtaining a Google Maps API key, storing it in the application property curam.miscapp.geocode.googlemapsapikey, and activating/publishing the geo-code enabled property to trigger Google Geocoding API V3. Useful if you need setup steps for address geolocation or Google Maps integration.

children.title: Specifying the default latitude and longitude values for Google Maps
children.summary: Explains how to set the default center point for the Google map in Cúram by editing application properties as sysadmin under System Configurations > Shortcuts > Application Data > Property Administration. Use curam.intake.map.default.center.latitude and curam.intake.map.default.center.longitude, enter the desired latitude and longitude values, save each, and publish changes. Includes example Salt Lake City defaults: 40.768109 latitude and 111.901147 longitude.

children.title: Setting the service area
children.summary: This section explains how to set up map and service-area configuration in Cúram. It covers enabling geocoding, defining default Google Maps latitude/longitude values via application properties, and using the Add Zip Codes control on the service area pane to assign zip codes to a service area. Useful if you need to configure map display or location-based service area coverage.

children.title: Setting the geocoding bias area
children.summary: Explains how to set the geocoding bias area in Cúram. An administrator navigates to Administration Workspace > Shortcuts > Universal Access > Geocoding Bias Area, selects Edit, then uses the +/− icons or drags the map to include the desired location and saves. The section defines geocoding as converting addresses to geographic coordinates and mentions Google Maps APIs as related information.

children.title: Selecting the service
children.summary: This section explains how to associate a service with a service area in Cúram by entering the service’s reference value (for example, Shelter for Child Care). It appears alongside related setup tasks for adding ZIP codes to a service area and setting a geocoding bias area on the map, both done from Administration Workspace > Shortcuts under Universal Access. Useful if you need instructions for configuring service area coverage or geographic targeting.

children.title: Configuring Service Groups
children.summary: Explains how to configure a new service group in Cúram. As administrator, go to Administration Workspace > Shortcuts > Provider Management > Service Groups, create a new group, enter its name (example: Childgrp), choose applicable service categories such as Child Care, move them with the left/right arrows, and save. The section also briefly shows preceding steps for editing a service reference (example: Child Care to Shelter) and begins the next topic on enrolling providers.

children.title: Enrolling providers
children.summary: Explains how to enroll a provider in Cúram. The section instructs resource managers to first confirm the provider is not already enrolled by entering at least one search criterion and running Search, then complete the mandatory fields in the Enroll Provider pane and enter the provider’s primary address. Useful for users performing provider registration and needing the basic enrollment workflow.

children.title: Enrolling provider groups
children.summary: How to enroll a provider group in Cúram. It instructs a resource manager to open Providers and Services > Shortcuts > Provider Group > Enroll Provider Group, first search to confirm the group is not already enrolled, then enter the group’s details in the Enroll Provider Group pane. It notes that the mandatory fields must be completed and the provider group’s primary address should be entered.

children.title: Adding the service and approving providers
children.summary: Explains how to add a service to a provider and approve the provider in Cúram. From My Providers, a resource manager selects a provider, opens the Services tab, creates a new service, and searches for the desired service (example: Child Care). The section also continues prior provider group enrollment guidance, noting required enrollment fields, use of the primary address for Google Maps, and a warning not to duplicate an already enrolled provider group.

children.title: Adding the approved provider to the provider group
children.summary: Explains how to add an approved provider to a provider group in Cúram using My Provider Groups. The procedure is: open the target provider group, choose New Provider, search for the approved provider, select it, and save. After saving, the provider appears on the provider group home page in the Name column, confirming it is now part of the group.

children.title: Displaying Google Maps in internal triage
children.summary: Explains how internal triage displays Google Maps for eligible community services. After registering a person with a valid ZIP code and setting the geocoding bias area, an eligibility worker creates a new triage from Care and Protection, completes the script, and submits it. The Community Services tab then shows available services, service locations, and a corresponding Google map. Example scenario uses 128 Broadway, Salt Lake City, UT 84101, showing a Child Care service location.

Explains how to configure Google Maps for internal triage in Cúram: enable geocoding with `curam.miscapp.geocode.enabled`, set a Google Maps API key or Business API credentials, and publish the property. Also covers setting default map center latitude/longitude, service areas, geocoding bias area, service references, service groups, provider enrollment, and approving providers. The final workflow shows triage results displaying community services and a Google map for eligible clients. Useful for setup or troubleshooting map display in internal triage.

This section defines triage in CCI and how its answers, captured through an IEG script, are evaluated by CER to produce recommended community services, providers, government programs, and urgent alerts. It also covers provider map behavior after triage/screening, including preselected results limits, location-based filtering, default map centering, boundaries, and zoom. The latter pages explain how to enable and configure Google Maps/geocoding, set default coordinates and bias areas, enroll and approve providers/services/groups, and display map results for eligible internal triage clients.

children.title: Defining Screening
children.summary: Explains how CCI configures screening and application intake. Screening can be single or multiple; multiple screening begins with category selection, while single screening goes directly to program selection. Screening questions are captured through IEG scripts, configured in IEG administration, and eligibility/alerts are produced by CER rules on submission. The section also introduces application configuration: agencies can support one or many applications and programs, with configurable sub-processes. It outlines capture and submit application setup, including single program/single application and other multi-program/multi-application options.

children.title: Defining Applications
Capture Application
children.summary: Explains the Capture Application function in CCI. It provides infrastructure to run one or more IEG application scripts, with IEG administration used to configure the application questions and structure. The section sits within broader application configuration, where agencies can support single or multiple applications/programs. It introduces submit-application options such as single program/single application, multiple programs/single application, and multiple programs/multiple applications, with a simple single-program case used as the baseline.

children.title: Submit Application
Single Program/Single Application
children.summary: Explains the simplest CCI application setup: a single agency, one program, and one application. This configuration is used when the agency is not collaborating with another agency and only administers one program, such as subsidized child care assistance. It sits within the broader discussion of screening and application configuration options, including single vs. multiple screenings and single vs. multiple programs/applications.

children.title: Multiple Program/Single Application
children.summary: Explains CCI application intake configurations for agencies managing multiple programs. Covers a single combined application for multiple programs, including the option to create separate submitted applications with unique references from one submission. Also describes multiple program/multiple application intake with pre-populated duplicate data to support “No Wrong Door.” Includes routing applications to work queues or specific users, and how submitted form data, clients, evidence, and application date are recorded and mapped to cases/evidence records.

children.title: Multiple Program/Multiple Applications
children.summary: Explains the multiple program/multiple applications intake model: agencies can handle different programs on separate application forms, and duplicate data may be pre-populated across forms to avoid re-entry, supporting No Wrong Door. Also covers routing applications to work queues or specific users based on channel, program, or configured allocation rules, and recording submitted application details, evidence, and mappings to case data, with configurable application date defaults.

Describes CCI application submission configuration options for agencies applying for one or more programs. It introduces three intake models: single program/single application, multiple programs/single application, and multiple programs/multiple applications. The excerpt explains that single program/single application is used by a non-collaborating agency with only one program, such as subsidized child care assistance. This section sits within broader application setup guidance, covering how agencies configure application scripts and submission handling.

children.title: Route Application
Route Application to Work Queue
children.summary: Describes how CCI routes and records submitted applications. For work queues, applications can be sent by channel, program type, or combined application type, using configurable work queues. Applications can also be routed to a specific user, with fallback allocation if unavailable. The section also explains recording application details, clients, and evidence; configuring application date defaults; and mapping application questions to case evidence and participant records.

children.title: Route Application to User
children.summary: This section explains how applications are handled after submission in CCI. It covers routing to either a work queue or a specific user, including examples such as channel-based and program-based queues, automatic assignment to a user’s My Applications list, and fallback routing if the user is out of office. It also briefly notes recording submitted application details, configurable application dating, and mapping application questions to cases, evidence, and participant records.

This section explains how CCI routes submitted applications and records their data. Applications can be sent to work queues by channel, program type, or combined application type, or assigned directly to a specific user with fallback routing if unavailable. It also covers recording application details, clients, and evidence, configuring the default application date, and mapping application questions to case, evidence, and participant records.

children.title: Record Application
children.summary: This section explains how CCI records submitted application data, including application details, clients, and evidence. It notes configurable application date defaults for online applications, either today’s date or based on local office business hours. It also describes how agencies can map answers from application questions to created case records, such as Income Support evidence and participant records.

children.title: Establish Existing Case
children.summary: This section describes configuration for establishing an existing case in CCI. It covers adding or transferring applications to existing cases, filtering case lists, configuring the application list and preview panel, mapping submitted application evidence to integrated cases, and managing evidence through multiple tools. It also explains configurable program recommendations, eligibility dates, eligibility result ordering, and CER rules. Finally, it covers setting application status routing and reopening denied/withdrawn programs, including limits, reasons, and intake timer updates.

children.title: Application List
children.summary: Describes configurable application-list behavior in CCI. The agency can control which applications appear on a person’s home page by application status/type, and whether a preview panel and its content are shown. The section also mentions related intake settings for establishing existing cases, evidence mapping/maintenance, program recommendations and eligibility dates, application status routing, and rules for reopening denied/withdrawn programs, including limits and reasons.

children.title: Maintain Evidence
children.summary: Explains how CCI maintains evidence after application submission: evidence captured on an application is mapped to the related integrated case via configurable data mappings. Evidence can then be managed through the evidence dashboard, edit evidence list, active evidence list, or verifications list. Useful for understanding agency configuration of application-to-case evidence flow and available evidence maintenance views.

children.title: Program Recommendations
children.summary: This section explains configurable program recommendation behavior in CCI applications. It covers which programs are included and their display order, how eligibility dates and decision-period dates are set, and that eligibility/entitlement rules are driven by CER rules. It also notes related application setup areas such as establishing existing cases, application lists, evidence mapping and maintenance, setting application status, and reopening denied/withdrawn programs with configurable limits and reasons.

children.title: Set Application Status
children.summary: Configures what happens when a user sets an application to “ready for determination,” based on role. For example, an intake worker may trigger routing of the application to the “applications awaiting determination” work queue. This section is about application status actions and workflow routing at status change.

children.title: Reopen a Program
children.summary: Configurable reopening of denied or withdrawn programs on an application. Agencies can choose which programs are eligible to be reopened, limit how many times reopening is allowed, and define allowable reopening reasons. If a program has an intake timer, denial or withdrawal completes the timer and sets its end date.

children.title: Manage Business Time Frames
children.summary: Configures business action timers for applications using Cúram milestone functionality. Agencies can set time periods, allow intake/eligibility workers to extend timers, and require approval for extensions. The section also covers application-to-case creation options: one or multiple applications can create one or multiple cases depending on agency setup. Finally, it explains when integrated cases become visible to workers, typically after at least one program is disposed, though earlier visibility can be configured.

children.title: Creating a New Case for an Application
children.summary: This section explains CCI configuration options for creating new cases from an application. It covers common patterns: one application to one program/one case, one application to multiple programs/one case, one application to multiple programs/multiple cases, and multiple applications to multiple programs/multiple cases. It also notes that agencies can control when the integrated case becomes visible to intake and eligibility workers, with the recommended setting being after at least one program has been disposed, though earlier visibility is configurable.

children.title: Notifying the Client of the Eligibility Determination
children.summary: This section explains how CCI notifies clients of eligibility decisions once an application is disposed, including approval details, benefit amounts, conditions, or ineligibility. It also introduces configurable life events: agencies can define event catalogs, categories, scripts, CER rules, and evidence updates routed to active cases. Finally, it outlines PCR setup, where agencies configure rules, weightings, event-driven or manual assessments, and case-type-specific ratings/classifications.

This section explains how CCI defines and configures applications: agencies can support single or multiple applications and programs, including single program/single application, multiple programs/single application, and multiple programs/multiple applications. It covers capture and submit application setup, routing applications to work queues or users, recording submitted details and evidence, application lists and preview panels, program recommendations, status changes, reopening denied/withdrawn programs, business timers, and creating new cases from applications, including when integrated cases become visible.

children.title: Defining Life Events
children.summary: This section explains how CCI supports defining life events for intake: agencies can configure the life event catalog, categories, descriptions, IEG question scripts, CER rules, and evidence updates to affected cases via the evidence broker. It also begins the PCR configuration section, covering rules that calculate PCR ratings/classifications from case and participant data, optional rule weightings, case-type setup, and whether PCR is triggered by events or manually entered by users.

children.title: Defining PCR
Rules
children.summary: This section explains CCI rule-based configuration for intake outcomes. It covers notifications of eligibility determinations, life event setup (catalogs, categories, IEG question scripts, CER rules, and evidence broker updates), and PCR rules. PCR rules use participant/case/evidence data and optional weightings to calculate case priority, complexity, and risk, can be configured by case type, and may be triggered by predefined events or manual user action.

children.title: Events and Manual Functions
children.summary: Describes how CCI can trigger PCR either automatically from configured intake events or manually by users. Agencies can define event-based PCR assessments at points like application submission or evidence activation, use predefined events, and allow manual PCR triggering or direct entry of PCR classifications. This section also sits alongside related intake configuration topics such as eligibility notifications and life event setup.

children.title: Classification
children.summary: This section explains configurable PCR classification settings. Agencies can define classification by application type, such as high priority, and configure value ranges for PCR-related measures like priority, complexity, and risk, either using one shared range or separate ranges by case type. Example ranges are Low 0–40, Medium 41–80, and High over 80. It also notes that PCR responses can display the rating and classification on the application home page and PCR tab.

children.title: Range
children.summary: This section explains PCR range configuration. It notes that PCR value ranges can be set for priority, complexity, and risk using the same or separate ranges, with a sample setup of Low 0–40, Medium 41–80, and High above 80. It also says ranges can be configured by case type. The page briefly mentions PCR classifications and that PCR ratings/labels appear on the application home page and PCR tab, then introduces referral configuration and points to Provider Management for community service referrals.

children.title: Response
children.summary: Describes PCR response display: the sample PCR rating and classification appear on the application home page and on the PCR tab for the application. Also notes that PCR classifications and ranges can be configured by application or case type, with sample low/medium/high ranges. Includes a brief transition to referral configuration, stating community service and external agency referrals can be configured, with community service referral setup managed in Provider Management (CPM).

This section introduces PCR configuration in CCI: rules use participant, case, evidence, or other application data, with optional weightings, to calculate overall priority, complexity, and risk, and can be set by case type. It also covers how PCR can be triggered automatically by predefined intake events (for example, application submission or evidence activation) or manually by users, including manual entry of PCR classification. The page also briefly notes eligibility determination notifications and life event setup infrastructure.

children.title: Defining Referrals
children.summary: Explains referral configuration in Cúram CCI: agencies can set up community service referrals and external agency referrals. Community service referral settings are managed in Provider Management (CPM), with a pointer to the Provider Management Guide for details. This section sits after PCR classification/range/response configuration and before the guide’s conclusion.

Defines how CCI intake is configured across triage, screening, applications, life events, referrals, and PCR, with functions assignable by user role. Explains triage via IEG questions and CER rules to produce recommended services, providers, programs, and alerts, plus provider map behavior and Google Maps/geocoding setup. Covers application routing, recording, evidence mapping, status changes, reopen/timer rules, case creation options, and notification of eligibility decisions. Also outlines life event catalogs/scripts/rules and PCR rules, triggers, and manual entry.

children.title: 1.11 Conclusion
Summary
children.summary: This summary concludes the guide by recapping CCI’s core intake features: a cohesive “No Wrong Door” approach for accessing community and government services, quick client search and registration, triage to find nearby services, screening for eligibility, application submission, reporting life events, and making referrals to community or external agencies. It also notes that agencies can configure their own eligibility, advisor rules, and intake functions. The page also mentions PCR classification/range configuration and where PCR responses appear in the application.

children.title: Additional Information
children.summary: This section includes the guide’s conclusion and a list of related Merative/Cúram documents for further reading (integrated case management, child services, participant, outcome management, universal access, evidence gathering, service planning, investigations, verification, evidence, provider management, evidence broker). It also contains legal notices: usage permissions, copyright/license terms, warranty disclaimers, export restrictions, privacy policy link, and trademark statements. It is mainly reference/legal material, not product functionality.

Conclusion to the Common Intake Guide. Recaps CCI’s main intake capabilities: “No Wrong Door” access, client search/registration, triage to nearby services, eligibility screening, application submission, life-event reporting, and referrals to community/external agencies. Notes agencies can configure eligibility, advisor rules, intake functions, PCR classifications/ranges, and where PCR results appear. The rest is reference material: related Merative/Cúram guides plus legal notices, copyright/licensing terms, warranty disclaimers, export/privacy links, and trademark statements.

Overview of Common Intake (CCI), a configurable intake framework for social enterprise agencies. It explains the “No Wrong Door” model for needs-based programs, covering client search/registration, triage, screening, applications, life events, referrals, and PCR-based prioritization. It distinguishes needs-based versus protection-based intake, outlines worker roles and configurable intake paths, and describes how agencies can tailor questions, rules, routing, alerts, and responses. The section ends with a summary, related guides, and legal/notices material.
